data.title: First Watch Restaurant: Brunch Boom, Earnings On The Side
data.description:
First Watch Restaurant Group (FWRG), the full-service daytime chain, blew past all the bearish chatter and delivered a strong quarter—stronger than even I expected.

That said, I did nail one thing: the market wasn't focused on same-store sales. It was all about the improved full-year guidance.

As I pointed out before, First Watch was up against easier comps this quarter and took a more measured approach to pricing (currently raising prices below inflation).

Perhaps resilience in Florida combined with softer traffic comparisons could propel it to low single digits for Q2.

In fact, this stole the show, since behind all this, something seems to have been forgotten: First Watch missed EPS estimates, missing by at least $0.02, reporting it at $0.03.

Well, this isn't a big deal, is it?

It depends. A few cents might not raise eyebrows at first, but once you realize they came at the cost of a 330 bp year-over-year drop in restaurant-level margins (now down to 18.6%), it starts to look a lot less harmless.

And keep in mind: as First Watch continues buying back franchises, it becomes even more exposed to rising operating costs. That's actually the main reason adjusted EBITDA fell 13.8% year-over-year (or $4.9 million), landing at $30.4 million.

Since my last post here in mid-July, First Watch shares have risen just over 1.4% and are rising by another low single digit in pre-market trading. This outperformed the S&P by a small margin, which was up 0.7% in the same period.

Highest Same-Store Sales Since FY 2023

In Q2 FY 2025, it felt like First Watch was riding the casual dining wave more than getting dragged down by the usual daytime dining headwinds.

If you’ve been keeping up with Goulart’s Restaurant Stocks lately, you’ve probably seen the signs pointing in that direction:

  1. Data from the NRA indicates that the restaurant industry improved sequentially from March to May after a very weak February. Of course, recent NRA data indicates that June likely represented a sequential slowdown compared to May (a 0.1% drop), but that doesn't matter much in the Q2 story;
  2. Casual dining led the 'Top Performers' in literally every month of the year (except for February, when fast-food came out on top and only led for one month, but with casual dining still topping the pack). This is easy to explain, they're simply showing better value-per-dollar than fast-food and fast-casual. Whether through fixed-price promotions, early-bird dining, or loyalty program discounts;
  3. Regions like California, Arizona, and Texas were the underdogs in the quarter, even though chains like Texas Roadhouse (TXRH) and Chili's (EAT) performed well there. The Mid-Atlantic and Southeast were particularly strong regions.

First Watch is technically a casual dining FSR, with a strong footprint in the Mid-Atlantic and Southeast—even though Texas is actually its second-biggest market.

But I still saw two issues: their price points are on the higher side, and they're focused on daytime meals.

Mixed feelings about that.

I wasn’t ruling out a traffic rebound, but I wasn’t fully sold either. After all, traffic had been falling for eight quarters in a row (two full years!) and the small drop of 0.7% year-on-year in Q1 already indicated that they were almost turning positive.

At the end of the quarter, First Watch reported same-store sales growth of 3.5% year-over-year, improving 280 bp sequentially.

Just take a look at how this played out over the quarters:

Breaking this down, we have traffic growing 2% year-over-year (improving sequentially by at least 270 bp) and average check per guest growing 1.5% year-over-year (up 10 bp sequentially).

I don't have data on the average check per guest breakdown, but I imagine this is a result of low-single-digit pricing year-over-year offset by a slightly negative mix. Ultimately, the average check grew below inflation, which shows they are keeping pricing stable.

Below is a compilation of First Watch's traffic and average check growth data from recent quarters:

You can see in the chart above that both traffic is facing increasingly easier comparisons, while there is a tendency for them not to stretch prices much, at least during FY 2024 and FY 2025. With traffic turning sideways, the trend is for average check growth to remain in the low single digits while traffic carries the brunt.

Pipeline on Fire, But Pay Attention to a Few Things

Beyond the traffic rebound, what really seemed to excite the market was the growth story behind this chain—which now has 600 locations across the country.

In addition to the classic franchise acquisitions they've been making for some time (often at low multiples, as I mentioned in previous posts), they've been opening many new restaurants. This quarter alone, 17 new restaurants were opened, with another 19 franchisees acquired (in markets like North Carolina, South Carolina, and Missouri).

The goal is bold and remains on the table: 62 to 67 restaurants open for the entire FY 2025.

To gain this momentum and partially circumvent the Trump tariffs, First Watch is leveraging the websites of other restaurant chains that are closing to convert them into its own.

This is a strategy that other chains like El Pollo Loco (LOCO) are also implementing.

But when thinking about greenfield construction, consider a few things:

  • Last time I checked, building a First Watch required approximately $1.6 million—which is quite inexpensive considering it's an FSR. Legacy locations are less than 4,000 square feet, and are between 1,000 and 1,500 square feet smaller than the new locations they've been building recently. Taking this into account and adding a 10% pressure from tariffs, we can imagine the new units costing around $1.8 to $2 million;
  • They are currently generating AUVs of $2.3 million (about $700,000 above where they were in FY 2019). The long-term goal is to generate AUVs exceeding $2.7 million;
  • Restaurant-level margins have been a source of much discussion. With Food, Beverage, and Packaging costs, as well as labor, rising rapidly, First Watch reported them at 18.6% in Q2 FY 2025 (up from 21.8% in Q2 FY 2024). This gives us (assuming AUVs of $2.3 million) an annual unit-level EBITDA of $427,800.

This is interesting, as new locations are showing a return of between 21% and 23%, depending on construction costs, at this point, well below the 35% they're targeting in the long term.

If we consider the $2.7 million AUV they're aiming for, with current margins, we find a return of between 25% and 28%.

So, you can see that they clearly need to leverage these margins if they want to achieve the ROI they're aiming for.

But don't get me wrong, with what they're currently cooking (AUVs of $2.3 million and a restaurant-level margin of 18.6%), they come out ahead of many FSRs out there.

For example, if we consider the high-end construction costs ($2 million), we find a payback period of almost 5 years and an IRR of 21%.

This is light years ahead of Applebee's (7-year payback and 14.5% IRR), Bubba's 33 (TXRH) (6-year payback and 17.1% IRR), and even surpasses the lean locations of BJ's Restaurants (BJRI) (5-year payback and 19.5% IRR).

But it still lags behind names like Kura Sushi (KRUS), Cheesecake Factory (CAKE), and even STK (STKS).

So, it seems obvious to me: they need to reduce their cost centers if they want to keep greenfield expansion financially attractive.

To give you an idea, Food, Beverage, and Packaging represent 23.6% of total sales (an increase of 180 bp year-over-year).

There's pressure from raw materials like eggs (which are already dropping in price and should give the company some breathing room going forward), bacon, coffee, and avocados.

Pricing is a key piece of the puzzle here.

The only reason they're able to keep food costs under 30% is because they charge premium prices. But if commodity inflation keeps creeping up and they're forced to hold price increases in the low single digits, we could see those food costs spike a bit.

Not dramatically, though. Their premium positioning gives them enough cushion to keep it relatively in check.

In my opinion, labor costs are a more dangerous thing at the moment. They increased from 32.8% of revenue to 33.2% even with the company investing in AI workforce forecasting and a reservation system.

Wage inflation was 3.9%, above the period's pricing.

These two reasons make me skeptical about an improvement in restaurant-level margins at the moment.

But there's no denying they're growing—and fast!

Financial Recap

First Watch reported total revenue of $307.9 million for the quarter—up 19.1% year-over-year. This is the highest year-over-year growth since Q2 FY 2024, when revenue grew over 19.5% year-over-year.

Remember, not all of this came from same-store sales. In fact, the bulk came from the 149 restaurants (opened and not comparable or acquired from franchises) they've added to their portfolio since then.

As I mentioned earlier, adding more company-owned restaurants means you're taking on more operating risk. And unless you're running a tight ship at the unit level, that can really weigh on your bottom line.

That's exactly what happened here—restaurant-level margins dropped 330 basis points to 18.6%, and adjusted EBITDA came in at $30.4 million, down $4.9 million from the same quarter last year.

On the bottom line, they reported a diluted EPS of $0.03, much lower than the $0.14 they reported in the same period last year.

Management also updated guidance for all of FY 2025:

  • Positive single-digit percentage growth in same-restaurant sales, with flat to slightly positive growth in same-restaurant traffic;
  • Total revenue growth of approximately 20%, with a net impact of 400 bp from completed acquisitions. This means revenue for FY 2025 is estimated at $1.21 billion;
  • Between 62 and 67 new restaurants systemwide, including 55 and 58 new company-owned restaurants and 7 and 9 new franchised restaurants, with 3 planned company-owned restaurant closures;
  • Commodity cost inflation is projected for fiscal year 2025 to be in the 5% to 7% range, down from the previous high single-digit levels. Restaurant labor cost inflation is still expected to be in the 3% to 4% range;
  • Adjusted EBITDA to $119 million to $123 million (previous projection at $114 million to $119 million);
  • Capex between $148 million and $152 million (previously $150 million to $160 million).

Remember, they're basically telling us that both EBITDA is expected to be higher than expected, and there will be less pressure on Capex (likely to be used for acquisitions), leading them to issue more debt to cover the cash shortfall.

While improved, the EBITDA guidance of between $119 million and $123 million is still below what they had guided for at the end of FY 2024 ($124 million to $130 million), before lowering it in Q1.

Above $18: Is It Worth Buying?

In case you forgot, I've had a $16 price target on First Watch for a while now—anything in the $14s has looked like a pretty solid entry point, with an exit around $18.

But now that they’ve reviewed guidance again, it’s time to take another look at the numbers.

This time around, I'm running a different kind of comps model.

Let’s assume that three years from now, First Watch slows its unit growth, stabilizes margins, and starts delivering more predictable EBITDA gains. In that scenario, it wouldn't make sense to apply a high-growth multiple anymore. Instead, I'd peg it in the range of 12x to 14x EV/EBITDA — right in line with other mature FSR names.

In this sense, I'm considering EBITDA between $160 and $170 million for FY 2028.

This would give us exit prices between $18 and $25. Discounting this to present value at a 7.5% discount rate, we'd get a price target range between $14.50 and $20. The midpoint of this is $17.25, not far from where we were before.

Wall St. has a consensus price target of $21.50 on this stock, so even if you go with the bulls, the upside from here is just over 13%.

That's not exactly what I'd call an asymmetric bet, especially for a growth stock.

So, I'm sticking with my 'Hold' rating for now. I might nudge my price target up slightly, to somewhere in the $16.50 to $17.50 range, but that's about it. To be honest, I don’t see this stock breaking into the $20s anytime soon. I’d much rather wait for a pullback below $15 before revisiting my thesis from a valuation standpoint.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SoundHound AI: You Might Regret Buying More Now
data.description:
SoundHound AI: Scaling Up Its Automotive Growth Engine

Wait a minute. I asked previously where the breakout was. With the incredible surge that saw the stock of SoundHound AI, Inc. (NASDAQ:SOUN) notched a gain of more than 25% to close Friday's session, I think bullish investors might have just gotten what they asked for.

The Voice AI platform company has made highly encouraging strides to expand into new growth verticals beyond its core automotive offering. SoundHound AI has accelerated the growth of its automotive business, continually expanding the array of partnerships and bolstering the assessed multi-billion dollar TAM that it has targeted to scale over time.

In my previous SOUN write-up, I made it clear that I was confident in its credibility to continue ramping up its Voice AI opportunity against bigger rivals/big tech integrators. Yet, I also questioned whether the steep premium implied in SOUN's valuation still deserves a Buy rating?

Hence, I thought it's heartening to know that its most pivotal automotive business remains the key growth and monetization driver behind the recent stellar results. Not only does it lend due weight to the market's optimism, but I believe it also helps validate SOUN's value proposition on broadening its go-to-market into new direct or indirect verticals in the next few years.

Accordingly, SoundHound AI emphasized that the automotive pillar played a monumental role in its stellar 217% YoY revenue spurt, marking a new quarterly record for SOUN. It's crucial for us to consider that the foundational vector in SoundHound's market share gains in the monolithic opportunity still requires scaling with the range of auto OEMs.

Therefore, I believe the Voice AI company has demonstrated that credibly, as it indicated further tie-ups with another "three global brands," that incorporated its in-vehicle chat assistant technology stack in the US. Also, it has pursued its global expansion plans, as SOUN has also secured a partnership with a Chinese automaker that reportedly represents "millions of units powering a prominent auto brand that's winning and expanding globally."

Hence, I believe it goes to show that having a proprietary Polaris foundation model has proffered it with substantive differentiation advantages by helping to lower the costs of adoption while also improving the speed and accuracy (35% accuracy edge over peers and 4x reduction in latency). That should help position the third and potentially massive pillar in Voice commerce constructively to transform into one of SOUN's growth opportunities, leveling up its ARPU economics while also paving the way to accelerated profitability metrics over time.

SoundHound AI: Peak Growth Worries Still Dominate

Nevertheless, the company also noted that such deal momentum may not be replicated every quarter, reminding investors of the need to temper overoptimism. Also, while Wall Street has raised SOUN's near-term outlook in view of the company's upgraded guidance, analysts are still reticent in projecting similar growth momentum through 2026.

Although the path toward adjusted EBITDA profitability exiting 2025 remains on track, the company might have to demonstrate that it could cross-sell its monetization levers more quickly, with the market plausibly scrutinizing future updates from management on its commerce prospects.

Hence, I think it's opportune for us to be judicious about SoundHound's opportunity set while giving the company more time to justify its high-growth premium.

Buy Or Sell SOUN Stock?

In all honesty, SOUN appears to have approached and also retested the breakout zone at just under the $15 level. However, we must also appreciate the high volatility inherent in its price action, so it's quite challenging to ascertain an exact level to assess whether the breakout could prove decisive.

I must highlight that the stock's uptrend bias indicates that dip-buying cadence over the past four months has been incredibly resilient. And that goes to show that selling fervor is seemingly lacking, as if the bears have gone into hibernation (but could still reappear later with vengeance).

With SOUN's forward EV/revenue metric of almost 28x (way above tech median of 3x), do bulls really believe that they have a path to retake the stock's December 2024 peak?

Instead, from my vantage point, I assess that SOUN's buying momentum could still face significant hurdles if the market doesn't expect its Q2 revenue surge to be replicated in the second half. And, I think unless management can guide to a much improved profitability path, the right thing to do for now is to continue watching the developments from the sidelines.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: Boston Scientific Corporation Is Expensive But Worth It
data.description:
Overview

The other day, a colleague referred to an investment opportunity as a “forever company.” He intended to implant the notion that there exist certain stocks an investor should never sell in toto; i.e., it is okay to take some profits along the way and diversify. Despite an F Factor Grade for valuation from Seeking Alpha, we believe Boston Scientific Corporation (BSX) deserves our long-term buy rating for management’s foresight, strong balance sheet, highly profitable operations, commitment to R&D, and the company’s leading position in the development of AI-energized medical devices. The stock’s proven track record for growing one’s portfolio is admirable.

A forever stock like BSX boasts long-lasting quality, fostering growth and profitability. Its indefinite duration is due to management’s commercial foresight, investment in R&D, consistent development and marketing of new products, and building worldwide enduring partnerships. The company develops medical devices, procedures, and therapies. Leading products by medical specialties include

  • Critical Care products
  • Electrophysiology products
  • Female Pelvic Medicine products
  • Gastroenterology products
  • Gastrointestinal Surgery products
  • Gynecology products
  • Interventional Cardiology products
  • Interventional Radiology products
  • Neurological Surgery products
  • Otolaryngology products
  • Pain Management products
  • Pulmonology products
  • Urology products
  • Vascular Surgery products

Tailwinds to Higher Share Price

BCC Research claims the global medical device market is growing at a CAGR rate of 9.8% and will reach $1.3T by 2029. It will grow faster as AI advances, is used in medical devices, and will revolutionize diagnostics and treatments. Add the fact that the world’s population is aging, increasing the demand for more medical devices and treatment solutions. The U.S. Census Bureau reported on June 25, 2025, that “the U.S. population age 65 and older rose by 3.1% (to 61.2 million)” between 2023 and 2024. Boston Scientific’s integrated strategies, according to Klover.AI, put the company in a leadership position to monetize its strategies.

Klover.ai, an AI researcher, concludes

Boston Scientific (BSX) is poised to dominate the AI-enabled medical technology landscape through a deliberate, multi-pronged strategy that integrates advanced artificial intelligence directly into its core, high-growth product portfolios, particularly in cardiology. This product-led AI approach, fueled by robust financial performance, strategic acquisitions, and the cultivation of unique data and talent moats, creates a more defensible and synergistic competitive advantage than the platform-centric or less-focused strategies of its peers. The company’s strategy is built on four key pillars. First, a clear C-suite vision and exceptional financial results provide the mandate and capital for sustained, long-term AI investment. Second, BSX is developing and acquiring AI tools like HeartLogic, BeatLogic, and AVVIGO+ that are not standalone software but are deeply embedded in revenue-generating devices, enhancing their clinical value and creating a sticky ecosystem for healthcare providers. Third, a disciplined M&A and venture capital strategy systematically acquires proven AI technologies and nurtures early-stage innovation to fill pipeline gaps and accelerate market entry. Finally, BSX is cultivating two critical, hard-to-replicate assets: a world-class AI and machine learning talent pool and vast, proprietary, longitudinal datasets from millions of implanted devices, which are essential for training next-generation algorithms.

Valuation

On July 23, ’25, the company reported “another excellent quarter—marked by exceptional top-line performance—that delivered margin expansion and prioritized investment for future growth.” However, almost all the valuation metrics used by Seeking Alpha rate an F or D+ factor grade in comparison to the Health Care sector's medians. Meanwhile, more than 30 analysts assess the stock as a buy opportunity, though the shares are selling at $104 each, near their 52-week high of $108.94. The consensus is that the stock will outperform going forward.

A fair value price per share is $105 by multiplying the 35 PE ratio times EPS of $3. If the company beats the estimated EPS, the average target price can top $120 in the next two quarters, adding another 10% to 15% for momentum. Its 9-month price performance is up 22.5% compared to the sector median of -17.4% and up 43.68% for one year’s performance compared to -13% for Health Care companies’ stocks. Fintel puts the short interest float at a low 1.32% with 4.45 days to cover.

Q2 ’25 highlights from the financials are the 22.8% increase in revenue over Q2 ‘24; GAAP net income rose to $0.53 per share from the estimated $0.47 per share; and sales increased in each; the U.S. by 30.7%, in EMEA by nearly 7%, Asia-Pacific by about 16%, Latin America and Canada by 6% on average, and most importantly in Emerging Markets by about 12%. Cash from operations spiked each of the last 4 years and tops $4.285B TTM. Free cash flow per share is $2.37 TTM, up from $1.81 in Dec ’24.

Risks Warranting Buys on Dips

Some of the challenges facing Boston Scientific include investors’ demand for the company to consistently build the ROI through the implementation of its AI plan. The company’s growth in revenue is partly attributable to its aggressive M&A moves. There have been, on average, 2 acquisitions per year for the last 5 years, all doing business in the theater of operations as the core business of Boston Scientific; that is another indication of how management has been sticking to the knitting.

Insiders and hedge funds sold more shares than they purchased in the last quarter as the stock approached $100 per share. Funds sold 892,000 shares. Insiders sold $1.2M worth of shares in the last 3 months. However, more funds owned shares almost every quarter. In Q2 ’23, 76 funds reportedly owned BSX; 90 in Q2 ’24; and 109 at the end of Q1 ’25.

Headwinds the company can face include changes in regulations for medical devices from the USHHS, cybersecurity pressures, and production costs versus payment affordability. It is important to watch for impacts on income that Boston Scientific generates from Medicaid and Medicare, with cutbacks in federal programs expected to impact revenues by the end of 2025.

Takeaway

At the current valuation, with insiders selling shares, and threats to revenue from HHS cutbacks, retail value investors should act prudently and watch for buy opportunities on dips in the share price. Nevertheless, Boston Scientific appears to have a bright future in a growing industry, especially selling into Asia-Pacific and Emerging Markets. The next two quarterly reports are important to watch to see if the company can maintain its high gross profit margin (~68%) and revenue growth. Like Mark Twain's perspective, long-term growth is best achieved by embracing continuous improvement over perfection, accepting change as a part of growth, and recognizing that regret often stems from inaction rather than action.

data.title: Abbott Laboratories Q2 Earnings: Dividends Don't Lie
data.description:
ABT Stock: Previous Thesis And New Developments

I last analyzed Abbott Laboratories (ABT) in June 2024 with an article titled “Abbott Laboratories: Why I Agree With Wall Street On This One”. That article was triggered by the change in the prevailing sentiment around the stock at that time. I rated it as a buy based on these following considerations:

Wall Street analysts have a 'strong buy' rating on ABT stock, and we agree with such bullish sentiment. Despite some risks, ABT stock has the potential to outperform the overall market by a large margin. The key return drivers include its strong growth potential, high ROCE, and a discounted valuation.

Since then, a few material changes have emerged and motivated this follow-up coverage. First, the stock price has appreciated (and outpaced the broader market as seen in the chart below) substantially, calling for a re-examination of its valuation risks. Second, a few recent geopolitical events, most notably the tariff rate changes and the new Executive Order that President Trump signed to lower drug prices, have added considerable uncertainties to the healthcare sector as a whole. Finally, the company has also reported its latest earnings and operations, with a few notable updates. The goal of this follow-up article is to consider these changes and re-examine its reward/risk ratio. In the end, I keep seeing an attractive investment opportunity and reiterate my buy rating.

ABT Stock: FQ2 Earnings Report Recap

Let me start with the FQ2 earnings report [ER] that ABT recently released on July 17 (see the chart below). As seen, Abbott reported showed a normalized EPS of $1.26, which was in line with expectations for the quarter. Revenue was reported at $11.14B, slightly exceeding projections by $84.11M. Looking ahead to the upcoming quarter, the company's EPS is projected to grow mildly by about 3% sequentially to a normalized EPS of $1.30 and a GAAP EPS of $1.04. The latest revisions were dominated by downward revisions (19 vs. 3 upward revisions only).

I indeed see good reasons for the above sentiment judging from the updates provided in ER and also the macroscopic uncertainties ongoing (i.e., the potential of tariff changes and the drug price control). Looking closer at its FQ2 growth drivers, I consider the momentum in its Medical Device business to be leading one. Sales on this front increased 13% in the quarter on the back of double-digit gains in diabetes care, heart failure, and structural heart. All of these could be subject to drug price scrutiny and also tariff rates. Furthermore, FQ2 results also showed weakness in its Diagnostics segment (-1%). This largely reflects continued pressure on COVID-19 testing-related sales and volume-based procurement programs in China, which is another area that could be sensitive to tariff and trade disputes.

ABT Stock: Latest Payout Declaration

Past experiences have taught me that the more uncertainties there are, the more I must focus on return vectors with the least amount of ambiguity. As a long-established dividend aristocrat, I could not think of a vector that is more reliable than ABT’s dividend payouts.

In tandem with its FQ2 ER, Abbott also declared its third dividend payout for 2025, as you can see from the following table. The company has declared a regular quarterly dividend of $0.5900 per share with an ex-dividend date of July 15, 2025. This translates into an annual dividend growth rate of 7.2%, a quite healthy growth rate in my view.

ABT Stock: Valuation

In terms of valuation, the P/E ratios now exhibit a range of grades due to the sizable price appreciation since my last writing. For example, the Non-GAAP P/E [TTM] is now 26.95, which is a substantial 66% higher than the sector median of 16.18 and also more than 7% higher than its 5-year average of 25.17. Similarly, the FWD P/E (on a non-GAAP basis again) is also substantially (by more than 50%) higher than the sector median of 25.68 and also about 4% above its own historical average.

However, if you subscribe to my earlier notion that ABT’s dividends represent a much more reliable earning metric than accounting EPS, dividend yield then is a more reliable metric for assessing ABT's valuation risks than P/E ratios. P/E ratios, especially those based on non-GAAP, are susceptible to being skewed by accounting adjustments, one-time gains or losses, and a company's specific reporting choices.

While dividend payouts have none of these ambiguities. Turning to the dividend yield chart shown below, I see the stock at a fair valuation. To wit, the current dividend yield is 1.76%, which is only slightly below the 10-year average of 1.81%. Also note that its yield has fluctuated historically between a quite tractable range, spending most of its time between 2.5% and 1.2%, as seen.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

Before closing, let me revisit the issues of macroscopic uncertainties. My view is that the impact of the Trump administration’s plan to lower drugs is difficult to quantify at this stage with the amount of specifics available. But the impacts from a few other macroeconomic headwinds seem to be substantial based on the information provided in its Q2 ER. For example, my estimate points to ~$700 million top-line headwind due to the fading COVID-19 test demand and pricing pressure from China. The country buys medical products from ABT in bulk at significant discounts. Based on the information the company provided in the Q2 ER, I also expect ~$200 million in tariff-related costs for FY 2025 based on tariff rates at that time. Since then, the Trump administration has proposed to place 250% tariffs on pharmaceuticals if the industry could propose a satisfactory plan to lower drug prices.

To close, the goal of this ABT follow-up is to re-examine the reward/risk profile given the new catalysts surrounding the stock since my last writing. These main catalysts considered are the valuation expansion since my last writing, the ongoing tariff and trade disputes, and finally the potential of drug price changes. I certainly see some near-term challenges. However, I do not see any of these issues fundamentally altering Abbott’s long-term moat – best reflected by its healthy dividend growth in my model. The underlying moat across its core business segments remains intact, in my view. I also expect the moat to be further augmented by a cycle of new product launches from its pipeline in the coming years. Finally, dividend yield suggests the stock is trading near a fair valuation too. With these considerations, I will conclude with a reiteration of my buy rating on the stock.

data.title: Lucid Motors Is Not Dead Money
data.description:
It was only a few weeks ago that I covered Lucid Group, Inc. (LCID) due to the company striking a partnership with ride-sharing company Uber Inc. (UBER) that signified the entry of the EV company into the autonomous vehicle market.

Lucid Motors just released earnings for its second quarter that showed another bad quarter in terms of losses and cash burn. Unfortunately, the EV company also trimmed its outlook with respect to anticipated electric vehicle deliveries in 2025. The reduction in the delivery forecast triggered an 8% price slump for the stock of Lucid Motors on Thursday.

My last stock classification for Lucid Motors was ‘Buy’ predominantly because I considered the Uber and Lucid deal a significant event in the autonomous vehicle industry.

I think that Lucid Motors, particularly in the context of the Uber partnership, deserves the benefit of the doubt, and I am not modifying my stock classification, despite a lower anticipated delivery volume this year.

Burgeoning Losses, Trimmed Delivery Forecast For 2025

Lucid Motors’ second quarter sales rose 29.3% YoY to $259.4 million, which fell short of the Street’s estimate of $262.4 million. The electric vehicle company also reported a non-GAAP loss of $0.24 per share, which missed the Street estimate by 2 cents.

Lucid Motors continued to exhibit high losses, driven by an attempt to improve the scale of its vehicle manufacturing, which resulted in a net loss of $855.3 million in 2Q25. Losses continued to rise as well, by 8% compared to the second quarter in 2024, thanks to higher investments in R&D, but also higher costs associated with the scaling of the Lucid Gravity EV that came to market at the end of last year.

Unfortunately, Lucid Motors’ EBITDA and free cash flow trend do not look much better. The electric vehicle company produced negative EBITDA in the amount of $632.1 million and lost more than $1.0 billion in free cash flow in the June-quarter, as the company scaled up new production. Lucid Motors’ deliveries surged 38% YoY in the last quarter, and the EV company delivered a total of 3,309 electric vehicles to its customers as Lucid Gravity EV production increased.

Widening losses are primarily a negative for the investment thesis from a sentiment point of view because it will take the electric vehicle company longer to achieve breakeven. To put the numbers from Lucid Motors’ second quarter earnings presentation into perspective, the EV company lost about $4 in free cash flow for every $1 it produced in sales.

Though high losses related to production growth are to be taken seriously, Lucid Motors has a very strong liquidity position and recently signed a deal with Uber that will see the ride-sharing company invest $300 million into the electric vehicle startup. Even without this investment, Lucid Motors is doing rather well.

According to the company’s 2Q25 balance sheet, the EV company had $2.8 billion in available liquidity in its bank accounts, including short-term investments. Including long-term investments, Lucid Motors’ total liquidity was $3.6 billion.

Since the Saudi Sovereign Wealth Fund is a major investor in the company (affiliates own about 60% of the EV company’s outstanding stock and have repeatedly participated in new stock sales), Lucid Motors has another stable liquidity source to tap if it needs additional funding to scale up its production and deliveries in the coming years.

With negative free cash flow of more than $1.0 billion just in the last quarter, and total free cash flow losses in the first half of the year summing up to $1.6 billion, it is to be anticipated that the EV company will turn to more stock sales in order to bolster its balance sheet as it scales deliveries of the Lucid Gravity EV.

In terms of the forecast for 2025, Lucid Motors slightly softened its outlook for 2025 and now anticipates 18,000-20,000 electric vehicles to be delivered to its customers compared to a forecast of 20,000 electric vehicles in the prior quarter. The new forecast anticipates between 76% and 95% YoY growth.

Lucid Motors Could See A Stock Re-Rating

U.S. electric vehicle companies, perhaps with the exception of Tesla Inc. (TSLA), are selling for moderately low price-to-sales ratios, which is a reflection of them having quite challenging operating fundamentals at the present stage of their developments.

Most EV companies are still losing a lot of money, Lucid Motors being one of them, but the long-term outlook is not nearly as bad as the Lucid Motors/Uber/Nuro deal has showcased.

The partnership is poised to result in Lucid Motors delivering 20,000 electric vehicles in the next six years to Uber, with potential for additional orders if the robotaxi experiment bears fruit.

Tesla is selling, despite considerable delivery headwinds amid an ongoing consumer strike, for 9.0x next year’s estimates sales, whereas Rivian Automotive Inc. (RIVN) is selling for a 2026e sales multiple of 1.9x, the lowest such multiple in the electric vehicle peer group.

Lucid Motors is selling for a price-to-sales ratio of 2.9x, and the calculation assumes a 2026e YoY sales growth rate of 100%, making the electric vehicle company the fastest-growing U.S. EV brand (in terms of sales) in the peer group in the next year.

Lucid Motors’ risk/reward relationship is skewed to the positive, in my view, as the recent Lucid Motors/Uber/Nuro deal opens up a new way for the EV company to make money: selling EVs to ride-sharing companies. This could potentially lead to sustained sales and delivery growth tailwinds for Lucid Motors.

Why Lucid Motors Might Disappoint Investors

Lucid Motors has quite disappointed from an investment perspective in the last couple of years, and the company’s market valuation and stock chart reflect that.

Besides the reduced delivery forecast, the ‘One Big Beautiful Bill’ Act passed into law last month, which means that tax credits for the purchase of electric vehicles are set to expire at the end of September.

No federal tax credits for new and used EVs will apply after September 30, 2025, which could potentially lead to sales, delivery, and margin pressures for electric vehicle companies, with smaller EV companies being particularly vulnerable.

My Takeaway

The stock price of Lucid Motors dropped 8% after the company made its second quarter earnings presentation on Thursday, which unfortunately included a downgrade in the electric vehicle company’s delivery forecast for 2025.

In terms of gross profits and free cash flow, Lucid Motors is losing too much money still, which in turn is creating anxiety about the company’s profitability. That being said, a mitigating factor here is that Lucid Motors has a lot of cash on its balance sheet and has the Saudi Sovereign Wealth Fund in its corner to help out with additional capital raises to fund increases in production capacity.

Though the EV company’s losses were higher than anticipated, I think that Lucid Motors still deserves the benefit of the doubt, particularly after the deal with ride-sharing company Uber last month. Buy.

data.title: Low Sugar Halloween Treats: APA Corporation
data.description:
Introduction

We think that APA Corp., (NASDAQ:APA) is in the process of righting the ship, and shares at current levels represent a compelling value. As noted below the company trades at an absurdly low EV/EBITDA multiple as compared with the cohort I've selected below-U.S. shale operators with international operations.

The market has shunned the company for most of the past couple of years, taking the stock down from the low $40's to as low as $15.00 after Liberation Day. The stock rebounded into the low $20's before selling off again in the tariff back and forth. APA has just made a triple bottom at $18.00, and then opened strongly higher earlier trading on Thursday, breaking through previous resistance around $20.00. A bullish sign for the stock. We think the worm has turned on APA, and the easiest path is higher even with WTI in the $60's.

With over $2.0 bn in asset sales in the last couple of years, the company is delivering on the deleveraging promised post-Callon. It now has a line of sight to get long-term debt to about 2X NTM EBITDA in the next several years organically, or quicker with additional portfolio high grading. Without committing to a specific date for achieving this target, the company thinks it can be done by 2030.

With this quarter the company has demonstrated that it can operate profitably across the cycle, fund its capex, pay down debt, reduce its share count, and fund a competitive dividend yielding 5.54% at current prices. Even Doug Leggate-a normal pretty hard-nosed analyst said as he began his questioning with, " John, this is starting to look a lot like a turnaround ." We agree.

We rate the company a strong buy at current levels and think with the levers the company has to pull-Permian, Suriname, Alaska, and Egypt, momentum will shift to the upside in coming quarters. Investors taking a position at near current levels should see steady income and rising capital appreciation.

The thesis for APA

Bear with me a moment. I know it's a bit early to be thinking of Halloween, but in the wee hours when I started editing this article it was on my mind. Hence the title. No...in my 70's, I don't go Trick or Treating, but with my kids in middle age, and their kids in their early twenties, it's about the only time children show up at our house. Our neighborhood gets mobbed by the little gremlins, and we love it. Tying this all together, a share of APA Corp. is currently about the same price as a big bag of candy from Walmart, (WMT). Food for thought for those special kids in your family. If you can keep them from trading it in for cash, the $1.00 dividend each share brings will keep them racing through their parents house on a sugar high. And, isn't that what Halloween is all about? Moving on.

The last few years, with a couple of brief wartime exceptions, have been tough on holders of APA common stock. Five years down the road have left the company's stock where it began. There are a lot of reasons for this, and you know them well-oil and gas prices, debt, more debt, asset write-downs, gas-weighting of inventory when gas was out of favor. And others. Like I said it's been tough. The big news now is some of the big drags on the stock-FID on Suriname, dead money assets, staggering debt, and concerns about the long term viability of the company are in the rearview. At 2X EV/EBITDA there's a lot of bad news discounted in the stock price.

The company is generating positive EBITDA, and clawing back previously approved capex, while maintaining output thanks to increased drilling efficiencies. Going forward the company expects lower F&D costs to be structural-

Following strong performance in the first half of the year, APA is outpacing its three-year cost reduction targets and now expects to achieve $350 million in run-rate savings in 2026, compared to its prior goal of year-end 2027. The company also increased its 2025 realized savings target by more than 50% from $130 million to $200 million and raised the year-end run-rate savings target from $225 million to $300 million.

Costs are coming down in the core Permian footprint as the company tightens spacing and reduces frac intensity-sand water volumes. This saves upfront money-capex, and bolsters the longevity of drilling inventory. Stephen Riney, President offered some pretty good color in what this shift in thinking meant for their Callon assets-

Callon had a $78 WTI oil breakeven price. In 2024, we lowered that to $61. Currently, in the Permian, we are running on average in the low 40s across the entire Permian in terms of a WTI breakeven oil price. In Midland Basin, we're running in the high 30s. And in the Delaware Basin, we're in the low 50s. And most of that Delaware Basin stuff is Callon. And so Callon has come down in 2023 from a $78 breakeven oil price to low 50s at this point. All of this is increasing net asset value and increasing inventory duration, as you alluded to. And while I say average well productivity is lower, I think it's a focus on average well productivity is actually not the right metric. You really have to focus on the spacing unit because wells in a spacing unit are interdependent. And it's the spacing unit that actually matters.

APA has gone from 8 rigs down to 6 in the Permian thanks to a "step-change in drilling efficiencies. It also promotes more complete recovery from the field from which a well produces. This counters the trend we've seen in recent years where companies widen spacing and do expensive mega-fracs to get high IP-30's. APA's strategy makes sense in an era of tight margins. The company also noted increased company owned SWD facilities that will reduce disposal costs. APA is also building DUC inventory in the Permian.

Moving to the other cash generating play-Egypt, the company is having success in a recently 34% expanded 7-mm acre concession. The driver for APA is the country has gone from exporting LNG to importing it. This puts an onus on maximizing domestic production where the needs are very dire. Just today the country signed a deal for $35 bn in gas from Israel's Leviathan field. With its three decades of experience in Egypt, spent mostly looking for oil and ignoring gas, the company has a ready list of places to drill. Reduced diesel use due to power projects was noted as contributing to lower LOE's for the quarter.

Longer-term Alaska looks pretty bright. CEO John Christman touched on the results of the Sockeye well. The company is reviewing seismic and logging information in preparation for further exploratory drilling to begin in the middle of 2026.

Notionally GranMorgu is expected to come online in mid-2028. It appears that this may be moving more quickly and production could be realized before that. At full name plate capacity GranMorgu will make 220K BOEPD. With its 40% post capex carry stake in the project, APA could see a cash flow uplift of $1.3 bn from the project factoring Brent at $75.00.

Q-2 and guidance

On $2.13 bn in revenues, APA beat EPS projections on the strength of cost controls and capital restraint with EPS of $0.87. OCF was $1.3 bn, a 30% uplift from Q-1, and nearly doubling YoY comparisons. The company reduced debt in the quarter by 15% organically, and by $850 mm with the proceeds of asset sales. It also returned $140 mm to shareholders in dividends and share buybacks.

The company is forecasting a full-year BOE of 457K BOEPD with capex (including $275 mm for GranMorgu) between $1.975-$2.025 bn. In gas trading operations-the company buys gas from companies that don't have takeaway and resells it, they project meaningful uplift to income. At current strip pricing, their full-year guidance forecasts about $650 million in revenues from trading operations, a $75 million increase from their May update. A nice little side business.

Company filing

Risks

Things can always go lower, but we think at 2X TTM EBITDA, and $23K per flowing barrel the stock is largely derisked at current levels. Simple math on 670 mm BOE of PDP reserves gives them an NAV of $23 bn, or roughly 4X APA's capitalization. There is no metric where APA is overpriced at current levels.

One note of caution. Their proved reserves are just under 1 bn BOE. Now that has nothing from GranMorgu booked, but at their current rate of production, that's a ~ 6 year runway. This is something Doug Leggate quizzed them on, and I thought John Christman, CEO had a fairly cogent response, consistent with what they were saying about the new Delaware basin development strategy-

I outlined how our Permian development strategy is evolving in a lot of areas now where we're drilling more wells per section with smaller fracs, and it's really a function of getting the cost down and being able to drive the capital efficiencies and where we are, we're in the process of characterizing all of our inventory and all of the upside zones in the Permian. I have seen what I'd call the core inventory and where we historically would have said to the end of the decade, I can tell you today, looking at what I would call core development inventory, we're now well into the 2030s with run rate in terms of existing pace and time.

Your takeaway

Pretty much however you look at the numbers, APA is a screaming buy for investors looking for competitive income; a 5.5% yield is nothing to sneeze at, and a high probability of growth if the upstream industry can just catch a teensy break here or there. SA doesn't have a Screaming Buy category, so we will just call it a Strong buy.

As debt and share count decline and cash flow increases, the EBITDA multiple should come up. A fairly paltry 3.5X would take the stock back into the $40's, and that's a pretty good return from present levels. It might take a year or two, but I think the worst days are behind them and things should improve. Meanwhile, you will get paid to wait. Don't blow it all on candy!

I added today incrementally to my fairly large position on Thursday, and would like to add more on dips toward the $19.00 level.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital Corporation Trades At A -6.25% Discount To NAV, While Producing A Double Digit Dividend Yield
data.description:
Earnings season is in full swing and Blue Owl Capital Corporation (NYSE:OBDC) just delivered their Q2 earnings. I am a big fan of business development corporations (BDC) as they serve lower and middle market companies which have traditionally been underserved by traditional financial institutions. One of the main attractions for BDC’s is that they historically have produced large dividend yields from their book of assets. OBDC delivered another solid quarter as their net investment income (NII) came in at $0.42 per share which exceeded the Q2 dividend of $0.39 by 7.69%, providing a strong coverage ratio. OBDC remained very active in Q2 as their new investment commitments totaled $1.1 billion and they had $1.9 billion of sales and repayments. I am still bullish on OBDC as its net asset value (NAV) is $15.03 and investors are able to add shares at a -6.25% discount while the annualized dividend yield is around 12.22%. I think investors are getting a lot for their money with shares of OBDC and heading into a lower rate environment. I don’t believe shares of OBDC will trade at a discount for much longer. OBDC is one of my favorite BDC’s as it produces the 3rd largest amount of NII from its peer group and has the ability to expand its originations as the cost of capital declines.

Following up on my previous article about OBDC

I have been bullish on OBDC for a while and since my last article was published in May (can be read here) shares have increased by 2.63% while its total return after accounting for the dividend is 5.38%. OBDC has underperformed the market as the S&P 500 has climbed 11.94% over this period, but OBDC isn’t a growth stock, and I am not expecting it to replicate the returns from an index fund. I was excited about where OBDC was trading back in May because I thought the Fed would have cut rates at the June or July FOMC meeting. I felt that the OBDC merger with OBDE was bullish for shareholders as it would enhance the overall portfolio while producing operational savings and increase the amount of NII being generated from its combined book of assets. The deal positioned OBDC for strong future performance and would allow OBDC to capitalize on future opportunities. I am following up with a new article after their Q2 earnings to discuss OBDC’s performance and why I am still bullish on the investment case after comparing their metrics across the peer group. I think that OBDC will continue to be a dividend producing machine and generate some capital appreciation in the 2nd half of 2025.

Risks to investing in OBDC

While I am still bullish on OBDC there are several risk factors to consider. As a BDC OBDC specializes in providing direct lending solutions to middle-market companies through different debt instruments including senior secured loans. Their portfolio has a significant amount of credit risk due to most of its assets being tied to lending capital to companies with smaller balance sheets. This increases the risk of defaults on loans, and in the event of an economic downturn its portfolio companies may not be well equipped to ride out periods of uncertainty. CME Group is expecting that the Fed could take interest rates down to 200 bps next year which could negatively impact OBDC’s floating rate issued debt if their loan originations don’t increase enough to offset the difference. There is also the risk of overall returns as OBDC is not positioned to outperform the market over longer investment periods. OBDC is an income play, and investors shouldn’t expect to beat the market just because the dividend has a double-digit yield.

OBDC announced bullish Q2 results which I believe positions them for capital appreciation in the 2nd half of 2025

OBDC is the 2nd largest BDC by net assets and market cap which provides a level of stability that is hard to replicate throughout the space. OBDC generated $0.42 of NII in Q2 which was an increase of 2.44% QoQ. This contributed to OBDC’s 10.6% annualized return on adjusted net investment income which marked the 12th consecutive quarter that OBDC has achieved this. The increase in NII was driven by a higher level of one-time repayment income of $0.05 which was approximately $0.03 per share above their 3-year average. OBDC generated $485.8 million in total investment income which put them in a position to declare a $0.02 supplemental dividend on top of the regular dividend of $0.37 per share in Q2. OBDC’s financial metrics put them in a position to declare a $0.37 3rd quarter dividend of $0.37 per share after they generated a 109% coverage ratio of the base dividend with the Q2 adjusted earnings. The coverage ratio was supported by a healthy spillover income of approximately $0.33 per share, which is equivalent to nearly a full quarter's worth of base dividends. The spillover income helps OBDC maintain the large dividend payment during volatile conditions. Investors are still getting a large book of assets for a discount of around -6.25% based on its NAV of $15.03. I think that Q2 showcased that OBDC can expand its earnings, and it has the capacity to deliver attractive risk-adjusted returns based on the fundamental strength of its portfolio.

OBDC has a strong portfolio that consists of 233 portfolio companies spanning 30 industries, with an aggregate fair value of $16.9 billion. The average investment that OBDC makes is $72.4 million, and their portfolio is heavily geared toward debt investments with first-lien senior secured debt making up 75.8% ($12.8 billion) of the portfolio. OBDC has 5.4% of its portfolio tied to second lien secured debt, and 2.2% is unsecured. Senior secured debt is my favorite lending instrument as it puts OBDC at the front of the line to recover their investment in the event of bankruptcy. One of the reasons why OBDC is able to generate such a large yield is because 97.6% of its debt investments are at floating rates. The weighted average spread over the base rate for floating rate debt was 5.8%, and the weighted average total yield of accruing debt and income-producing securities at fair value was 10.6%. I believe that this highlights OBDC’s investment strategy as they prioritize downside protection. The interest coverage for borrowers increased to 1.9x in Q2, which enhanced OBDC’s spread while the payment in kind (PIK) income decreased to 9.1% of total investment income from 10.7%. OBDC’s new investment commitments were $1.1 billion across 6 new and 19 existing portfolio companies. OBDC also experienced $1.9 billion in sales and repayments which resulted in net leverage settling at 1.17x as OBDC delivered on its deleveraging priority post-merger.

I believe that OBDC is strategically positioned for growth as its sourcing capabilities are enhanced by its scale across Blue Owl Capital and its broader credit platform which is critical for generating deal flow. OBDC is expanding into alternative credit, investment-grade credit, data centers, and digital infrastructure which will broaden its product offerings and provide financing solutions for many in demand investments. This should create many new origination opportunities for OBDC as the OBDE merger provided crucial incremental capacity. I believe that OBDC’s enhanced sourcing capabilities and platform scale will continue to drive larger amounts of earnings from increased origination levels in a rate environment that is declining. As interest rates decline, I believe that M&A activity will pick back up and the larger BDCs will scoop up many new deals that the smaller BDCs won’t be able to facilitate.

OBDC looks undervalued compared to its peers, and I am bullish on its future

I track 12 different BDCs and am invested in 8 of them. I always compare these BDCs across their NAV, how much NII they produce, and what the dividend yields are. The 11 BDCs that I compare OBDC against are:

  • Ares Capital (ARCC)
  • Prospect Capital Corp. (PSEC)
  • Barings BDC (BBDC)
  • Main Street Capital (MAIN)
  • MidCap Financial Investment Corporation (MFIC)
  • Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD)
  • Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (OCSL)
  • Golub Capital BDC (GBDC)
  • FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK)
  • Gladstone Capital (GLAD)
  • Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL)

I look at the NII very closely because this tells me how much investment income after expenses a BDC's portfolio is generating. OBC is generating the 3rd largest amount of NII from its peer group which helps them distribute a double-digit yield. Since NII is where the dividends are paid from I want to make sure that the BDC is generating a large amount of NUU and that I am paying a good multiple for it. BDC’s are not known for their capital appreciation, so I want to put myself in the best position I can. OBDC is trading at a NII multiple of 9.48x which is slightly higher than the peer group average of 8.71x. OBDC should trade at a premium based on the amount of NII it generates, and I am happy to grab shares at a valuation that is less than 10x NII.

The NAV is very important because this indicates what price you’re paying for the book of assets on a per share basis. OBDC’s NAV is $15.04, and its share price is currently trading at a -6.25% discount which is a bargain in my opinion. I would pay up for OBDC, but when it's trading at a discount I tend to add more shares because of how strong the financial metrics are and how big the dividend yield is.

BDC’s are generally looked at as dividend investments, so not only do I want to pay the best price for a BDC’s book of assets, but I want to get a strong dividend yield from it as well. OBDC has a dividend yield of 12.22% which is above the peer group average of 11.97%. As the Fed lowers rates, I believe that capital will flow back into the equity markets from the sidelines as they won’t be able to replicate the amount of yield they were used to generating. I think that OBDC is in a great position as its been able to pay a supplemental dividend on top of the base dividend since Q4 2022. Heading into a lower rate environment I feel that OBDC is going to attract a lot of income investors looking for yield.

Conclusion

OBDC just reported their Q2 earnings, and I am shocked that their still trading at a discount to NAV which has pushed the dividend yield past 12%. I think that OBDC is undervalued, and I am planning to add to my position as there is still a large gap between its NAV and share price. When the Fed lowers rates, I believe we will see increased loan originations as the cost of capital declines. OBDC is in a position where they can offer better terms than many BDCs and can facilitate large loans for middle market companies looking to expand. I am surprised that there is still this large of an opportunity in shares of OBDC, and I am excited to see what happens over the next several months as Jackson Hole is coming up and the FOMC is a month and a half away.

data.title: Visa: Speculations Are Over, Buying Diversified IT And Finance In One (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
This February, I published the last review of Visa (NYSE:V) in the article Time To Sell Visa: Waiting For The Price To Drop For A New Long-Term Position. At that time, my rating of the company was sell, but despite the fact that the described scenario was fully implemented, the research left a dual impression on both the esteemed readers and me. The fact is that 3 months earlier, I published the first coverage of the company, which was quite bullish. And the last sell rating was formed due to a number of circumstances and was essentially speculative. I understand that the full picture was only in my head, and judging by the comments, it was not bought by the readers of Seeking Alpha.

2 quarterly reports since the last rating, having revised the system of assessing companies for a longer term, I upgrade Visa's rating to buy, based on a quantitative analysis of financial indicators and fundamental prerequisites for further business growth.

Thesis Disclaimer

I use a buy rating, but my approach would be better served by a rating scale from avoid to attractive. Attractive status or strong buy rating relative to V stock is based solely on an analysis of the financial performance, taking into account the current valuation as an additional factor. My opinion may be useful to investors considering whether to include the stock in a long-term portfolio and hold it for a 3-5 year horizon, and does not constitute a recommendation or trading instruction.

Valuation Context

When I published my last review, I based it on the current price of $351 per share, and warned of a possible correction of 14% to $301.8, which, for me, was a fair value based on a quantitative approach using the historical multiples method.

The scenario came true, and I returned to the ranks of the bulls. My quantitative approach involves fixing the trailing P/E TTM for the last 12 quarters on the day after the report is published. It should be noted that the market valuation of Visa is quite stable, and the spread of estimates is not large over this period: from 26x to 33x. This determines the proximity of both the median of the entire series of values ​​(which I take as the fair value) and the median of the upper and lower thirds of this sample, which serve as a benchmark for overvaluation and undervaluation for me.

Given the confident growth of EPS at the moment, all three levels have moved up, and the current fair value according to my method is $325.3, which is only 3% lower than the closing price as of Aug. 05. After touching the previous level for buying $301.8, the market gave a profit of more than 20%, but I will leave all speculations on the topic of speculation outside the review this time. The current 30x are in the market and leave an opportunity for medium-term and long-term investors for a long time.

Fundamentals

In Q3 FY25, international payments again showed double-digit growth of +16% YoY, especially due to tourist flows to Europe and Asia. This drives net revenue and outpaces the growth of the total payment volume.

Along with the steady growth of the cross-border segment, Visa is expanding B2B payment solutions (Visa Direct, Commercial Pay) and "value-added services" (fraud detection, data analytics, etc.) - together they already provide 60% of revenue and are growing faster than the main business segment of consumer payments. During Investor Day 2025, the company's management spoke about the importance of these areas and the opportunities that the company receives by developing them.

The last 2 quarterly reports only confirm the trend towards diversification and the focus on B2B & VAS in the context of C2B saturation.

The growth of the VAS and New Flows segments reduces the business's dependence on the cyclicality of consumption, transforming the company from a classic card network business into a multi-level payment platform with a high share of marginal areas. Every dollar of growth should be due to highly profitable services, not large-volume transactions.

The sustainable growth of cross-border transactions may be a temporary result of the recovery of international tourism after the pandemic, but it also provides greater stability for future financial results.

Quantitative Side

Against the backdrop of a changing revenue structure and shifting emphasis, it is interesting to see how financial indicators are reacting to this. Visa, based on the results of Q3'25, still has the most powerful financial performance metrics. In my first review, I think I formulated my understanding of the business very successfully:

The absence of R&D and low capital expenditures minimizes the risk of uncontrolled write-offs, making Visa an attractive blend of IT and financials with strong growth potential.

This allows the company to demonstrate record high or close to them values of net profit margin of 52% and ROIC of 40% (own calculations).

The company also has a high level of solvency, spending only 2% of operating profit on interest expenses (net interest expenses are positive). The cash flow from operating activities TTM of $23.b completely covers the total debt of $22b, calculated on the average for 4 quarters.

Ultimately, using my system for rating each company, I calculate an overall rating based on 8 financial metrics, and for Visa, it is 80% out of 100%.

Only one parameter (even with the greatest weight) deviated from the benchmark to the downside-this is the expected EPS growth against the historical one. If we rely on analysts' forecasts and the expected EPS of $14.46 by the end of September 2027, then the CAGR for the next 2 years will be ~12%, which is less than the actual one over the past 3 years (14.71%). Let the numbers be double-digit, and the deviation is not great, but I am strict in following the system, and for this, I deducted points from the overall rating.

Although I have a strong feeling that EPS will be higher. Firstly, the company has been regularly exceeding market expectations for 4 years in a row. Without exception.

Secondly, the last $30b buyback program has been authorized since April 2025. The program is almost half-fulfilled; stable FCF and low CapEx allow for full implementation, which supports EPS. Therefore, when I look at future growth rates, I write down "minus", keeping "plus" in mind.

Bottom Line

Visa remains one of the most reliable, attractive, and unique stocks in my portfolio. A mix of IT and finance, a high level of indicators at a valuation of ~30x makes it a high-quality long-term investment with a clear opportunity to manage the position based on the historical multiples method. Like any other, this idea has its risks. Antitrust prosecution and regulatory pressure are a permanent risk for a giant like Visa. For example, the adoption of the Credit Card Competition Act (CCCA) discussed in the US Congress in its current form may lead to a decrease in interchange fees and increased competition from banks and fintechs. Sustainable penetration of new payment technologies may collide with competition from real-time payment systems, especially in emerging markets. But weighing these risks and the arguments above, I choose a long-term buy.

data.title: Arbor Realty: I Own The 8% Yielding Preferreds Over The Commons
data.description:
Arbor Realty's (ABR) dividend stability and growth had for years rendered the mortgage REIT one of the best tickers in the space. However, a recent dividend cut and slipping coverage have kept me neutral on the common shares even as I continue to build my position in the preferred shares. The mREIT generated fiscal 2025 second-quarter revenue of $68.73 million, down 21.9% from its year-ago comp and a miss by $8.29 million on consensus estimates. The dip needs to be contextualized. ABR's structured loan portfolio had an unpaid principal balance ("UPB") of $11.61 billion as of the end of the second quarter, but was down 2.2% from $11.87 billion a year ago. Further, total delinquencies came in at $529 million as of the end of the second quarter. This is defined as loans greater than 60 days past due and loans where ABR is not recording interest income on but are less than 60 days past due.

The first tranche of these non-performing loans ("NPL") constituted 89.2% of second-quarter NPLs at $471.8 million spread across nineteen NPLs. This dipped from $676.2 million spread across 24 loans a year ago. ABR last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.30 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution and annualized at $1.20 per share for a 10.3% dividend yield. This is lower than the trailing-twelve-month yield of 13.62%. Critically, ABR generated distributable EPS of $0.25 during the second quarter, down 3 cents sequentially and a drop from EPS of $0.45 a year ago. This means the dividend is only being 83% covered during the second quarter, or a roughly 120% payout ratio. I last covered ABR in May with a neutral rating, flagging the dividend cut and uncertainty around coverage as reasons to stay muted on the commons.

Originations, Weighted Average Yield, And Dividend Coverage

ABR is not pulling back on the origination of new multifamily-backed loans even as some of its core geographical markets go through some weakness. The UPB of the mREIT's structured loan portfolio had originations of $716.5 million during the second quarter against runoff of $519.7 million. This meant net funding of $197 million. The weighted average yield on the UPB of the structured loan portfolio at 7.95% as of the end of the second quarter had also increased by 16 basis points from 7.79% a year ago. Hence, ABR continues to display strength in overall origination volume even as its NPLs remain elevated but down from its year-ago comp and as originations maintain positive net fundings.

The risk here is that slipping coverage portends another dividend cut. The mREIT's trailing-twelve-month free cash flow as of the end of the second quarter stood at $342.23 million, a deficit of around $27.77 million from the trailing-twelve-month total dividends paid of $370 million. ABR's management stated during their earnings call that they're set to start growing earnings and the dividend again from 2026 as they work through resolving NPLs. The year-over-year drop is encouraging, but the lack of common dividend coverage keeps me on the sidelines.

The Preferreds And Widening Credit Spread Risk

Rents have been falling across the U.S., with 23 metro areas experiencing falling rents in June. This dip has been more pronounced in states like Texas, which forms ABR's most salient geographic market. I have a large position in the 6.25% Series E Preferreds (NYSE:ABR.PR.E) to maintain exposure to ABR's sprawling multifamily-backed loan portfolio. I like the comfort of sitting higher up the capital stack to hedge the risk of possible reductions to my income.

The Series E, like the other two preferreds, at $17.50 per share is trading at a 30% discount to its $25 per share liquidation value. This discount slims to 28.5% for owners of the Series D (NYSE:ABR.PR.D), slimming further to 11.6% for the fixed-to-floating rate Series F preferreds (NYSE:ABR.PR.F). Crucially, the Series E pays out a $1.5625 per share annual coupon for a 9% yield on cost. While this is roughly 230 basis points less than the dividend yield on the common shares, it's 377 basis points more than the current U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y). Hence, they face the risk of a widening of credit spreads if the U.S. were to fall into a recession or broad economic weakness. However, the odds of a US recession are falling. JPMorgan (JPM) initially placed this at 60% in April, dropping to 40% in May. U.S. real GDP also expanded by 3% in the second quarter of 2025, beating expectations and likely eliminating the likelihood of a recession in 2025. I'm confident in the ability of the mREIT to work through NPLs in the long term, but the commons are not a clear buy at this stage. This is being rated as a hold.

data.title: Forget The S&P 500, Here's Where I'm Investing Instead
data.description:
The S&P500 (SPY) has been on a hot streak.

Since early 2020, it has more than doubled investors' money, earning a ≈15% average annual return:

REITs (VNQ), on the other hand, have done very poorly during the past five years. Including dividends, they have delivered only slightly positive total returns, but ignoring dividends, they have been in a quasi-bear market for the last five years:

As a result, lots of investors are today happily investing in the S&P 500 and ignoring REITs.

But this ignores that the last five years have been highly unusual. We had the pandemic, a historic surge in interest rates, and a huge tech tailwind from the AI revolution. The S&P 500 saw its P/E multiple expand significantly, whereas REITs saw their valuation multiples compress to historic lows.

Extending the time period to 50-plus years, we find that REITs have actually outperformed the S&P 500 and even tech stocks:

I think that going forward, REITs are likely to regain the lead because their valuations have dropped to the lowest level in a decade relative to the S&P 500, and they should enjoy significant capital flows as interest rates are cut down to lower levels:

With that in mind, let's look at a few REITs that could potentially earn far higher returns than the S&P 500 going forward. They have all massively outperformed the past, and we expect those superior returns to continue.

EastGroup Properties (EGP)

EastGroup Properties has been one of the best-performing industrial REITs over the long run, massively outperforming even the S&P 500:

It has done so well because it owns a Class A portfolio of last-mile, high-barrier-to-entry warehouses and distribution centers in rapidly growing markets.

These properties have greatly benefited from the growth of e-commerce, the growing trend of onshoring, and rapid population growth in the sunbelt over the years.

This has allowed it to grow its rents, all while developing new properties, earning large spreads over its cost of capital.

And we think that the REIT is well positioned to keep outperforming going forward.

Firstly, the fundamentals of its properties remain strong. Some sub-sectors of the industrial market are today overbuilt, but EGP specializes in smaller urban properties with high barriers to entry. Those have seen little new supply in recent years:

But while the supply is limited, we expect the demand for these properties to increase as the AI revolution takes hold. We expect it to accelerate the growth of e-commerce as it will make digital marketing so much more powerful, and we expect it to also boost the trend of onshoring, as AI and robotics reduce the cost of domestic supply chains.

EGP's properties are also ideally located in rapidly growing markets, with 60% of its revenue coming from Texas and Florida:

EGP's balance sheet is also historically strong with just a ~10% LTV, leaving ample capacity to take on new debt to develop additional properties as opportunities present themselves.

As a result, analysts expect the REIT to keep growing at a high single-digit growth rate for years to come, reaching $12.47 of FFO per share by 2029.

Yet, today, you can buy it at a near 10-year low valuation, trading at just 17x its current FFO and 12.8x its 2029 forward FFO. It's even cheaper than at the bottom of the COVID crash right now.

Just for context, it traded at 35x FFO in early 2022 before the start of this REIT bear market. Incredible what a difference a few years can make!

35x FFO might have been a bit much, but 17x is also too low for a REIT with so little leverage, such high-quality assets, and solid long-term growth prospects.

We estimate fair value at closer to 25x FFO, and we expect it to get there as interest rates return to lower levels and capital flows back to the REIT sector. This has the potential to unlock 30% upside, and while you wait, you earn a 3.5% dividend yield, which, coupled with an 8% annual growth rate, should be plenty to earn double-digit total returns.

That's exactly what the REIT has delivered for 30-plus years in a row, and everything indicates that this streak should continue.

Sun Communities (SUI)

Sun Communities is one of our favorite residential REITs at High Yield Landlord, and it has also significantly outperformed the broader market over the long run, including the S&P 500.

It specializes in manufactured housing communities and RV parks, providing affordable housing solutions in desirable locations to its residents.

We think that it is particularly well-positioned right now after recently selling its marina business to Blackstone (BX). This transaction has allowed it to significantly deleverage its balance sheet and positions it for faster growth going forward:

Even then, the REIT currently trades at a historically low valuation, trading at just 17x FFO, which is a particularly low multiple for a manufactured housing REIT.

For context, it traded at 33x FFO prior to this bear market.

Once again, 33x was likely a bit rich, but 17x is too low. Fair value is likely somewhere in the middle for such a high-quality REIT with low leverage and bright prospects. The management has been funding buybacks with a portion of the proceeds from the recent asset sales to take advantage of this opportunity. We see fair value at closer to 25x FFO, meaning that the stock has about 30% upside potential.

While you wait, you earn a 3.5% dividend yield, which is growing rapidly. The REIT just recently hiked its dividend by 11% and also announced a special dividend.

Between the yield, the growth, and the upside, investors can expect to earn 12-15% average annual returns for years to come, which is in line with what they have achieved over the past decades.

Risk

The main risk to the thesis is that interest rates surge to higher levels. In that scenario, these two REITs could underperform the S&P 500, at least in the near term, as REITs have been highly correlated to interest rates over the past few years.

The debt market is today predicting significant rate cuts within the next year, which only strengthens our thesis, but if you disagree and expect interest rates to rise, then you may want to limit your exposure to REITs.

Closing Note

The S&P 500 has been hot lately, but past recent returns are not an indication of future performance. On the contrary, the strong recent performance could come at the cost of future results as the P/E multiple cannot keep expanding indefinitely.

REITs, on the other hand, are setting the stage for a strong recovery following many years of underperformance. This recovery could happen already in the near term as interest rates are cut, and we are positioning ourselves at High Yield Landlord to profit from this opportunity.
